statementLines: No money bill can be introduced in Assembly of a State, except on the recommendations of-
questions.questionText: No money bill can be introduced in Assembly of a State, except on the recommendations of-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. A money bill can be introduced in the **state legislative assembly only and that too on the recommendation of the governor**.
A money bill is a bill that deals with financial matters like taxation, public expenditure, borrowing, and custody of the states funds. A money bill cannot be introduced in the legislative council, which is the upper house in those states that have a bicameral state legislature.
Every money bill is considered to be a government bill and can be introduced only by a minister.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Which one of the following is not correct with regard to the elections to the Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not correct with regard to the elections to the Lok Sabha?

options.a: The elections are on the basis of adult suffrage.
options.b: The Election Commission is required to conduct the elections.
options.c: The electoral rolls are prepared under the direction and control of the President of India.
options.d: On the request of the Election Commission, the President is required to make available to the Election Commission such staff as may be necessary for the discharge of its functions.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The preparation of electoral rolls for the Lok Sabha elections lies with the **Election Commission of India**, an autonomous constitutional authority.
They are responsible for the preparation and revision of electoral rolls, delimitation of constituencies, and the overall administration of the electoral process.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Which one of the following terms does not appear in the Constitution of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following terms does not appear in the Constitution of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The Constitution refers to the budget as the annual financial statement. In other words, the term **budget has nowhere been used in the Constitution**. It is the popular name for the annual financial statement that has been dealt with in Article 112 of the Constitution.
The Consolidated Fund of India is mentioned in the article 266. While, article 114 deals with the Appropriation Bill.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Which one of the following statements about Rajya Sabha is correct?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements about Rajya Sabha is correct?

options.a: It is not subject to dissolution.
options.b: Its members are chosen by direct election from territorial constituencies in the States.
options.c: It is required to choose, as soon as may be a member of the Rajya Sabha to be the Chairman thereof.
options.d: The Attorney General of India does not have the right to speak in Rajya Sabha.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Unlike the Lok Sabha, which is subject to dissolution, the **Rajya Sabha is a permanent body and is not dissolved**.
The Rajya Sabha is a continuing chamber, and its members have a fixed term of six years. One-third of the members retire every two years, ensuring continuity in the functioning of the house.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Which one of the following expenditures is not charged on the Consolidated Fund of India?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following expenditures is not charged on the Consolidated Fund of India?

options.a: Salary and allowances of the Chief Justice of India
options.b: Salary and allowances of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India
options.c: Salary and allowances of the Prime Minister of India.
options.d: Salary and allowances of the Chairman of the Union Public Service Commission.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Salary and allowances of the Prime Minister of India is not charged on the Consolidated Fund of India**.
It is made from the Consolidated Fund of India. **

Important Tips The budget consists of two types of expenditure-the expenditure charged upon the Consolidated Fund of India and the expenditure made from the Consolidated Fund of India.
- The charged expenditure is non-votable by the Parliament, that is, it can only be discussed by the Parliament.
- The expenditure made from the Consolidated Fund of India has to be voted by the Parliament. **Charged Expenditure**: Salaries and allowances of the President; Chairman and the Deputy Chairman of the Rajya Sabha and the Speaker and the Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha; the judges of the Supreme Court; Comptroller and Auditor General of India; chairman and members of the Union Public Service Commission, etc.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part XV: Elections
questions.microTopic: Elections, Political Parties, Anti-Defection Law
statementLines: Which one of the following is not covered by the Anti-Defection Law?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not covered by the Anti-Defection Law?

options.b: Large defections in a Party in various spells.
options.c: Smaller defection in a single spell.
options.d: Party members in Parliament casting vote either to topple or support a government.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **Anti-Defection Law**, also known as the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution of India, was enacted in **1985** to prevent the floor crossing of legislators.
Floor crossing is the act of a legislator switching from one political party to another, often in order to gain political advantage. The Anti-Defection Law prohibits legislators from defecting from their party unless they have the support of two-thirds of the members of their party.
This means that **large defections in a party in various spells are not covered by the Anti-Defection Law**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following pairs is not correctly matched?

options.c: 73rd Constitutional Amendment Act: Reservation of seats for women in the election of local bodies in urban areas

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **73rd Constitutional Amendment Act, 1992**, which came into force on April 24, **1993**, provides for the reservation of not less than one-third of the total number of seats for women in the Panchayats at all levels, as well as the reservation of one-third of the offices of Chairpersons of Panchayats at all levels for women.
Option a is correct: **Hindu Marriage Act: 1955** is an Act that regulates the marriage and divorce among Hindus, Buddhists, Jains and Sikhs.
Option b is correct: **Hindu Succession Act: 1956** is an Act that codifies the law relating to intestate succession among Hindus, Buddhists, Jains and Sikhs.
Option d is correct: **Sati (Prevention) Act: 1987** is an Act that prohibits the practice of sati, the burning or burying alive of widows, and its glorification.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Which is the only State in India to have the Common Civil Code?
questions.questionText: Which is the only State in India to have the Common Civil Code?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Goa is the only state in India to have a Common Civil Code**. Unlike the rest of India, which follows different personal laws based on religious affiliations, Goa has a uniform civil code for all its residents.
**

Important Tips
- **Goa Civil Code,** was inherited from the Portuguese colonial era and is applicable to all residents of the state irrespective of their religion.
- It covers matters such as marriage, divorce, inheritance, adoption and maintenance.
- It has some special provisions for different communities, such as allowing bigamy to Hindu men under certain conditions.
- **Uniform Civil Code** is mentioned in **Article 44** of the Indian Constitution as one of the directive principles of state policy.

statementLines: At which of the following stages general discussion on a Bill takes place in the Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: At which of the following stages general discussion on a Bill takes place in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. During **second stage**, the bill receives not only the general but also the detailed scrutiny and assumes its final shape.
Hence, it forms the most important stage in the enactment of a bill. **

Important Tips **Ordinary Bills:** Every ordinary bill has to pass through the following five stages in the Parliament before it finds a place on the Statute Book **Stage 1: First Reading**
- An ordinary bill can be introduced in either House of Parliament.
Such a bill can be introduced either by a minister or by any other member. No discussion on the bill takes place at this stage. **Stage 2: Second Reading**
- During this stage, the bill receives not only the general but also the detailed scrutiny and assumes its final shape.
This stage involves three more sub-stages, namely, stage of general discussion, committee stage and consideration stage. **Stage 3: Third Reading**
- At this stage, the debate is confined to the acceptance or rejection of the bill as a whole and no amendments are allowed.
**Stage 4: Bill in the Second House**
- In the second House also, the bill passes through all the three stages, that is, first reading, second reading and third reading.
**Stage 5: Assent of the President**
- Every bill after being passed by both Houses of Parliament either singly or at a joint sitting, is presented to the president for his assent.
A bill becomes an act after his assent.

statementLines: Union Parliament may legislate on any subject of State List-
1. To Implement International Agreements
2. By consent of State
3. When Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.
4. In national interest and after a resolution passed by Council of states with 2/3 majority.
Choose the correct option using codes given below:
questions.questionText: Union Parliament may legislate on any subject of State List- 1. To Implement International Agreements 2. By consent of State 3. When Proclamation of Emergency is in operation. 4. In national interest and after a resolution passed by Council of states with 2/3 majority. Choose the correct option using codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Union Parliament may legislate on any subject of State List in following cases;** (a) To Implement International Agreements;
(b) By consent of State; (c) When Proclamation of Emergency is in operation; (d) In national interest and after a resolution passed by Council of states with 2/3 majority.
Hence, all the options are correct. **

Important Tips **Parliamentary Legislation on subjects in State List:** The Constitution empowers the Parliament to make laws on any matter enumerated in the State List under the following five extraordinary circumstances:
- **When Rajya Sabha Passes a Resolution (Art. 249)**
- If the Rajya Sabha proposes with a two-thirds majority of its members present and voting that it is necessary or beneficial in the national interest, the Parliament can legislate on any of the subjects listed in the State List.
- **During a National Emergency (Art. 250)**
- It provides power to Parliament to legislate with respect to any matters in the State List if a Proclamation of Emergency is in operation.
- **When States Make a Request: (Art.252)**
- It provides power to Parliament to legislate for two or more States, when respective State legislatures pass a resolution in this regard.
- **To Implement International Agreements (Art. 253)**
- The Parliament can make laws on any matter in the State List for implementing the international treaties, agreements or conventions.
This provision enables the Central government to fulfil its international obligations and commitments.
- **During Presidents Rule (Art. 356)**
- When the Presidents rule is imposed in a state, the Parliament becomes empowered to make laws with respect to any matter in the State List in relation to that state.

statementLines: Match list-I with list-II and
select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists:
List -I
A. Public Accounts Committee
B. Committee on Petitions
C. Joint Committee on Stock Market Scam
D. Departmental Committees List-II
1. Ad hoc Committee
2. Standing Committee
3. Financial Committee
4. Functional Committee Code:
questions.questionText: Match list-I with list-II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the lists: List -I A. Public Accounts Committee B. Committee on Petitions C. Joint Committee on Stock Market Scam D. Departmental Committees List-II 1. Ad hoc Committee 2. Standing Committee 3. Financial Committee 4. Functional Committee Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The functions of the **Public Accounts Committee** are to examine the appropriation accounts and the finance accounts of the Union government and any other accounts laid before the Lok Sabha.
It is **Financial Committee**. The **Committee on Petitions** is an **Functional Committee**. **Joint Committee on Stock Market Scam** was an **Ad hoc Committee**. The committee recommended sweeping changes in stock market regulations.
**Departmental Committees** are **Standing Committee**. The main objective of the standing committees is to secure more accountability of the Executive (i.e., the Council of Ministers) to the Parliament, particularly financial accountability.
**

Important Tips
- **Parliamentary committees are of two kinds-Standing Committees and Ad Hoc Committees.**
The former are permanent (constituted every year or periodically) and work on a continuous basis, while the latter are temporary and cease to exist on completion of the task assigned to them.
- On the basis of the nature of functions performed by them, standing committees can be classified into the following six categories 1. Financial Committees 2. Departmental Standing Committees (24) 3. Committees to Inquire 4. Committees to Scrutinise and Control 5. Committees Relating to the Day-to-Day Business of the House.
6. House-Keeping Committees or Service Committees (i.e., Committees concerned with the Provision of Facilities and Services to Members): **Ad hoc committees** can be divided into two categories, that is, Inquiry Committees and Advisory Committees. 1. **Inquiry Committees** are constituted from time to time, either by the two Houses on a motion adopted in that behalf, or by the Speaker / Chairman, to inquire into and report on specific subjects.
2. **Advisory Committees** include select or joint committees on bills, which are appointed to consider and report on particular bills. These committees are distinguishable from the other ad hoc committees in as much as they are concerned with bills and the procedure to be followed by them is laid down in the Rules of Procedure and the Directions by the Speaker / Chairman

statementLines: Which one of the following is the period for the Legislative Council to detain the ordinary bills?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is the period for the Legislative Council to detain the ordinary bills?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **ordinary bill passed by the State Assembly can be delayed by the Legislative Council for a maximum period of four months**.
- If the bill is passed by the Legislative Assembly and sent to the Legislative Council, the latter can either pass it, reject it, or not take any action on it within **three months**.
If the Legislative Council rejects the bill or does not pass it within three months or passes it with amendments not acceptable to the Legislative Assembly, the bill is considered again by the Legislative Assembly, which can pass it again with or without amendments and send it back to the Legislative Council.
- The Legislative Council then has to pass the bill within **one month** or it is deemed to have been passed by both houses in the form in which it was passed by the Legislative Assembly.
- Therefore, the final power of passing an ordinary bill lies with the Legislative Assembly and the **Legislative Council can only delay it for four months at most**

statementLines: Which of the following are exclusive powers of Lok Sabha in comparison to the Rajya Sabha?
1. A Money/Finance Bill can be introduced only in the House of the People.
2. Regarding the rejection or amendment of the Money Bill.
3. The accountability of the Council of Ministers.
4. Concerning the creation of one or more All India Services.
Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following are exclusive powers of Lok Sabha in comparison to the Rajya Sabha? 1. A Money/Finance Bill can be introduced only in the House of the People. 2. Regarding the rejection or amendment of the Money Bill. 3. The accountability of the Council of Ministers. 4. Concerning the creation of one or more All India Services. Select the correct answer from the code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct-As per**Article 109**of the Indian Constitution, a**Money Bill can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha**and not in the Rajya Sabha.** The Rajya Sabha can make recommendations on the Money Bill, but it cannot initiate or introduce such bills.

**Statement 2 is correct-The**Rajya Sabha does not have the power to reject or amend a Money Bill**.** It can only make recommendations to the Lok Sabha, which may or may not accept those recommendations. Ultimately, it is the Lok Sabha that has the final authority over the Money Bill.
**Statement 3 is correct-The**Lok Sabha has the exclusive power to hold the Council of Ministers accountable**.** Members of the Lok Sabha can question the ministers, participate in debates, and pass resolutions to express their dissatisfaction or lack of confidence in the government.
The Rajya Sabha does not have this power to hold the Council of Ministers directly accountable.

**Statement 4 is incorrect-The**power to create All India Services**, such as the Indian Administrative Service (IAS) and the Indian Police Service (IPS), is**not exclusive to the Lok Sabha**.** It is a shared power between the Union (Central) Government and the State Governments. **

Important Tips
- **Money Bills** are specifically defined in the Indian Constitution under Article 110 and pertain to taxation, government expenditure, borrowing, etc. Money Bills can only be introduced in the Lok Sabha, the lower house of Parliament.
- **Finance Bills** have a broader scope and can be introduced in either the Lok Sabha or the Rajya Sabha (upper house), but they require the Presidents recommendation for introduction in the Rajya Sabha.

statementLines: 'Zero Hour' is the contribution of which country to the Parliamentary System?
questions.questionText: 'Zero Hour' is the contribution of which country to the Parliamentary System?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Zero Hour** is an informal device available to the members of the Parliament to raise matters without any prior notice.
The zero hour starts immediately after the question hour and lasts until the agenda for the day (ie, regular business of the House) is taken up.
In other words, the time gap between the question hour and the agenda is known as zero hour. It is an **Indian innovation** in the field of parliamentary procedures and has been in existence since **1962**. Unlike the question hour, the zero hour is not mentioned in the Rules of Procedure.

statementLines: The sitting of House of the People may the terminated by:
questions.questionText: The sitting of House of the People may the terminated by:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The sitting of the House of the People (Lok Sabha) can be terminated through **adjournment, prorogation, and dissolution**.
Adjournment is done by the **Speaker of the Lok Sabha**, while prorogation and dissolution are carried out by the **President of India**, as prescribed in **Article 85(2)** of the Constitution.
Note-The adjournment of the House means the suspension of the sitting of the House till the following or some later day or hour of time. **Adjournment sine die** means termination of the sitting of the House without specifying or fixing any definite date for its next sitting.
**

Important Tips
- **Adjournment** refers to the temporary suspension of the sitting of the House. The House can be adjourned sine die, which means that it is adjourned for an indefinite period of time, or it can be adjourned to a specific date.
- **Prorogation** refers to the termination of the sitting of the House for a fixed period of time. The House is prorogued by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister.
- **Dissolution** refers to the termination of the House of the People and the calling of fresh elections. The House of the People can be dissolved by the President on the advice of the Prime Minister

questions.questionText: Who has the right to issue money from the Consolidated Fund of India?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Consolidated Fund of India** is a fund to which all receipts are credited and all payments are debited.
No money out of this fund can be appropriated (issued or drawn) except in accordance with a **parliamentary law**. Hence, **Parliament** has the right to issue money from the Consolidated Fund of India.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Which of the following is not a Social Act?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is not a Social Act?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **MISA Act**, or the Maintenance of Internal Security Act, was a controversial law passed by the Indian parliament in **1971** that gave the administration of Prime Minister Indira Gandhi and Indian law enforcement agencies very broad powers, including indefinite preventive detention of individuals, search and seizure of property without warrants, and wiretapping.
It was **not a social act**. The other options are all social acts:
- The **Anti-Dowry Act** refers to the Dowry Prohibition Act, which was enacted in **1961** to prohibit the giving or taking of dowry.
- The **Protection of Civil Rights Act** was enacted in **1955** to prescribe punishment for the preaching and practice of Untouchability and for the enforcement of any disability arising therefrom.
- The **Prevention of Immoral Traffic Act** refers to the Immoral Traffic (Prevention) Act, which was enacted in **1956** to provide for the prevention of immoral traffic

**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Contingency Fund** is placed at the disposal of the president, and he can make advances out of it to meet unforeseen expenditure pending its authorisation by the Parliament.
Hence, **President can spend from Contingency fund even before authorization of Parliament**. But it shall be authorised by the Parliament later on.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Article 330** of the Constitution of India provides for **reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes in the Lok Sabha**.
The number of seats reserved for Scheduled Castes in the Lok Sabha is proportional to the population of Scheduled Castes in India. **

Important Tips-The **104th Constitutional Amendment Act** extended the reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes (SCs) and Scheduled Tribes (STs) in the Lok Sabha and state assemblies.

- **Article 331**: Presidents power to nominate two members from the Anglo-Indian community to the Lok Sabha.
- **Article 332**: Reservation of seats for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in the legislative assemblies of the states.
- **Article 333**: Representation of the Anglo-Indian community in the legislative assemblies of the states.

statementLines: Who is the leader of the Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: Who is the leader of the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **leader of the Lok Sabha is the Prime Minister**. The Prime Minister is the leader of the party that has a majority in the Lok Sabha.
The Prime Minister is responsible for the formation of the government and for leading the government in the Lok Sabha. **

Important Tips-The Constitution does not provide a specific procedure for the selection and appointment of the Prime Minister.
- **Article 75** of the Constitution states that the President shall appoint the Prime Minister.
- As per the conventions of the parliamentary system, the President appoints the leader of the majority party in the Lok Sabha as the Prime Minister.
- If no party has a clear majority in the Lok Sabha, the President may exercise personal discretion in selecting and appointing the Prime Minister.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Joint Session of both the Houses of Parliament is concerned-
1. For the election of the President of India
2. For the election of the vice-President of India
3. To pass the Bill relating to the amendment of Constitution
4. To discuss and pass such a Bill where both Houses have different opinion Select your answer from the following code-
questions.questionText: Joint Session of both the Houses of Parliament is concerned- 1. For the election of the President of India 2. For the election of the vice-President of India 3. To pass the Bill relating to the amendment of Constitution 4. To discuss and pass such a Bill where both Houses have different opinion Select your answer from the following code-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Provision of joint sitting is applicable to ordinary bills or financial bills only**. When both Houses have different opinion on any ordinary bill, joint sitting can be called on for it. Ordinary bills are those bills which are outside the scope of Article 368 and can be passed with simple majority.
Whereas, to make any changes in the election of the President or Vice-President, or to pass the Bill relating to the amendment of Constitution, a constitutional amendment bill with special majority needs to be passed.
**But for any bill under article 368, there is no provision of joint sitting**.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States

statementLines: The Representation of States in Council of States is based on-
questions.questionText: The Representation of States in Council of States is based on-
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The representation of states in the Council of States, also known as the Rajya Sabha, is based on the **ratio of their population**.
This is determined by the principle of proportional representation in which the number of seats allocated to each state is proportionate to its population.
On the other hand, in USA, every state has equal representation in Senate or the Upper house.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Who among the following can impose the penalty under Section 20 of the Right to Information Act?
questions.questionText: Who among the following can impose the penalty under Section 20 of the Right to Information Act?

options.c: Central Information Commission or State Information Commission
options.d: High Court or Central Information Commission or State Information Commission

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. Under Section 20 of the Right to Information Act, **both the Central Information Commission and the State Information Commission** have the authority to impose penalties.
The Information Commissions at both the central and state levels are empowered to adjudicate on appeals and complaints related to the implementation of the Act.
They have the power to impose penalties on public information officers or any other person who has contravened the provisions of the Act, including withholding information, providing false or misleading information, or obstructing the flow of information.
The penalties imposed by the Information Commissions serve as a deterrent and ensure compliance with the provisions of the Right to Information Act.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: An appeal under Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 shall lie from any Judgement, sentence or order to the high Court:
questions.questionText: An appeal under Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989 shall lie from any Judgement, sentence or order to the high Court:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. According to the provisions of the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, **1989**, an appeal can be filed to the High Court from any judgment, sentence, or order.
This **appeal can be made on both factual grounds**, involving the interpretation of evidence and facts of the case, **as well as legal grounds**, involving the interpretation and application of laws.
The High Court has the authority to review and decide on both the factual and legal aspects of the case while considering the appeal.

questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: How many seats are reserved for Scheduled Castes in the Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: How many seats are reserved for Scheduled Castes in the Lok Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. There are currently **84 seats reserved for Scheduled Castes in the Lok Sabha**. This is as per the order issued by the Delimitation Commission in **2018**. The number of seats reserved for Scheduled Castes is proportional to the population of Scheduled Castes in India.
**

Important Tips-Currently, there are 41 seats reserved for Scheduled Tribes in the Lok Sabha.
- The maximum strength of the Lok Sabha, as envisioned by the Constitution, is 552 members.
- The Lok Sabha consists of 543 seats, with 530 members representing the states through by-elections.
- Approximately 24% of all Lok Sabha seats, out of the total 543, are reserved for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes, with 84 seats reserved for Scheduled Castes and 47 seats reserved for Scheduled Tribes.

statementLines: Who is empowered for imposition and realization of a collective fine under SC and ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989
questions.questionText: Who is empowered for imposition and realization of a collective fine under SC and ST (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, 1989
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **State Government** is empowered for imposition and realization of a collective fine under the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, **1989** In **1989**, the Government of India enacted the Scheduled Castes and Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act in order to prevent atrocities against SC/STs.
The purpose of the Act was to prevent atrocities and help in social inclusion of Dalits into the society

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Which Article of the Indian Constitution provides the provision of Vidhan Parishad in the State?
questions.questionText: Which Article of the Indian Constitution provides the provision of Vidhan Parishad in the State?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. **Article 171** of the Indian Constitution provides for the composition of the Legislative Council, also known as Vidhan Parishad, in the States of India.
This article lays down the provisions related to the establishment, membership, and other related matters of the Legislative Council in states where such a council exists.
The Legislative Council is the upper house of the state legislature, and its members are partially elected and partially nominated. **

Important Tips
- **Article 170** of the Indian Constitution deals with the composition of the Legislative Assemblies of the States.
- **Article 172** of the Indian Constitution pertains to the duration of the State Legislative Assemblies. It states that the Legislative Assembly of a State shall continue for five years from the date of its first meeting, unless dissolved earlier.
- **Article 173** of the Indian Constitution deals with the qualifications for membership of the State Legislature. It sets certain criteria that a person must meet to be eligible to be a member of the Legislative Assembly of a State.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Under the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989, which of the following is barred?
questions.questionText: Under the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act 1989, which of the following is barred?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Under the Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes (Prevention of Atrocities) Act, **1989**, **Anticipatory Bail is barred**.
This means that a person accused of committing an offence under this Act cannot apply for anticipatory bail. **

Important Tips Some important salient features of the Scheduled castes and scheduled tribes (prevention of atrocities) Act, **1989**, are following:
-
- **Denial of Anticipatory bail (Section 18)**.
- Grant arms licenses to STs and SCs (Rule 3v).
- **Section 19**
- Denial of Probation to convict.
- Creation of Special courts (Section 14).
- Appointment of Special Public prosecutors (Section 15).
- Creation of new types of offenses not in the Protection of Civil Rights Act **1955** or Indian Penal Code.
- Empowers the Government to impose collective fines (Section 16).
- Punishment for neglect of Duties by a Public servant (Section 4).

questions.subject: Polity

statementLines: Who was the First Speaker of Lok Sabha?
questions.questionText: Who was the First Speaker of Lok Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. **Ganesh Vasudev Mavalankar** was the first Speaker of the Lok Sabha. He was elected on 17 April **1952**, the day the Lok Sabha was constituted after Indias first general election.
He served as Speaker until his death on 27 February **1956**. Mavalankar was a respected leader of the Indian National Congress and a veteran of the Indian independence movement.
He was known for his impartiality and his ability to maintain order in the Lok Sabha. He was also a strong advocate of parliamentary democracy and the rule of law.
**

Important Tips First person to hold constitutional posts:
- **President of India**: Dr. Rajendra Prasad
- **Vice President of India**: Dr. Sarvepalli Radhakrishnan
- **Prime Minister of India**: Jawaharlal Nehru
- **Chief Justice of India**: Harilal Jekisundas Kania
- **Governor-General of India**: Lord Louis Mountbatten (prior to India becoming a republic)

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: All offences punishable under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 are:
questions.questionText: All offences punishable under the Protection of Civil Rights Act, 1955 are:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The Protection of Civil Rights Act, **1955** is an important law that aims to prevent discrimination and oppression based on caste and untouchability in India.
The act specifies that all offences under it are **cognizable and non-compoundable**. This means that the police can arrest the accused without a warrant and that the accused cannot be released on bail without the permission of the court.
The act was amended in **1976** to enhance the penalties for the offences and to make them summarily triable by a magistrate. This means that the magistrate can try the case quickly and without following the formal procedures of a regular trial

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: The Protection of Civil Rights Act extends to:
questions.questionText: The Protection of Civil Rights Act extends to:

options.b: The whole of India except the Scheduled areas
options.c: The whole of India except the State of Jammu and Kashmir
options.d: The whole of India except the Union Territories of Goa, Daman and Diu

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The Protection of Civil Rights Act **extends to the whole of India**. This means that the act applies to all the states and union territories of India, including Jammu and Kashmir, Goa, Daman and Diu, and the scheduled areas.
**

Important Tips The offences punishable under Protection of Civil Rights Act are:
- Enforcing religious disabilities, such as preventing a person from entering a place of worship or performing a religious service on the ground of untouchability.
- Enforcing social disabilities, such as denying access to a shop, public restaurant, hotel, place of public entertainment, public conveyance, or any occupation, trade or business on the ground of untouchability.
- Refusing to admit a person to a hospital, dispensary, educational institution, or any other public service on the ground of untouchability.
- Refusing to sell goods or render services to a person on the ground of untouchability.
- Other offences arising out of untouchability, such as insulting, intimidating, assaulting, or boycotting a person on the ground of untouchability.
- Unlawful compulsory labour, such as forcing a person to do any work which is an offence under the Indian Penal Code on the ground of untouchability.

statementLines: Consider the following statements with respect to qualification to become the member of Rajya Sabha and
select the correct answer from the code given below:
1. Should be at least 30 years of age
2. Should be registered as a voter on the list of voters in the state from which the candidate is to be elected
3. Should not hold office of profit under the state Code:
questions.questionText: Consider the following statements with respect to qualification to become the member of Rajya Sabha and select the correct answer from the code given below: 1. Should be at least 30 years of age 2. Should be registered as a voter on the list of voters in the state from which the candidate is to be elected 3. Should not hold office of profit under the state Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct-According to**Article 84**of the Indian Constitution, a person must be**at least 30 years of age**to be eligible for membership in the Rajya Sabha.**

**Statement 2 is incorrect-The requirement of being registered as a voter in the state from which the candidate is to be elected applies to the Lok Sabha (the lower house of Parliament) and not the Rajya Sabha.** Rajya Sabha members are elected by the elected members of the Legislative Assembly of the state, and **voter registration is not a qualification criterion** for Rajya Sabha membership.
**Statement 3 is correct-According to**Article 102**of the Indian Constitution, a person cannot be a member of Parliament if they hold any office of profit under the government of India or the government of any state. Holding an office of profit would result in disqualification** from being a member of the Rajya Sabha.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: Who prorogues the Vidhan Sabha of a State?
questions.questionText: Who prorogues the Vidhan Sabha of a State?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Governor** is the authority who prorogues the Vidhan Sabha of a State. Prorogation is the act of terminating a session of the Vidhan Sabha by an order of the Governor. **

Important Tips
- **Prorogation ends both the sitting and the session of the House**.
- Usually, within a few days after the House is adjourned sine die (without fixing a date for reassembly) by the Speaker, the Governor issues a notification for prorogation of the session.
- Prorogation does not affect the bills or motions pending in the House, which can be taken up in the next session

questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: How can the speaker of the Lok Sabha be removed?
questions.questionText: How can the speaker of the Lok Sabha be removed?

options.a: By a resolution of Lok Sabha passed by a two-third Majority of members present.
options.b: By a resolution of the Lok Sabha passed by a majority of all the members of the House.
options.c: By President on the advice of the Prime Minister.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. The **Speaker of the Lok Sabha can be removed by a resolution passed by a majority of all the members of the House**.
This means that a simple majority of all the members present and voting in the Lok Sabha is required to pass the resolution for the removal of the Speaker.
The procedure for the removal of the Speaker is governed by the rules and procedures of the Lok Sabha.

statementLines: In which assembly of States, money Bills are proposed?
questions.questionText: In which assembly of States, money Bills are proposed?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. A money bill cannot be introduced in the legislative council (the upper house in those states that have a bicameral state legislature).
It can be introduced in the **legislative assembly (the lower house) only** and that too on the recommendation of the governor.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: What is the consequence of the failure of Government in Rajya Sabha?
questions.questionText: What is the consequence of the failure of Government in Rajya Sabha?
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. The **failure of the government in the Rajya Sabha does not have any consequences for the government**.
The government can continue to function even if it does not have the support of the Rajya Sabha. The government is formed by the party or coalition of parties that has the majority in the Lok Sabha.
**

Important Tips The Lok Sabha can express lack of confidence in the government in the following ways-When government fails to pass No-Confidence motion.
- When Money Bill is not passed in the Lok Sabha.
- When Motion of Thanks is not passed in the Lok Sabha on the Presidents inaugural address.
- When Censure Motion for Adjournment Motion is passed.
- When Cut Motion is passed.
- When the government is defeated on vital issue

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: In any State of India, Legislative Council can be created or can be abolished by
questions.questionText: In any State of India, Legislative Council can be created or can be abolished by

options.a: The President on the recommendation of the Governor of the State
options.c: The Parliament as per Resolution passed by the Legislative Assembly of the State
options.d: The Governor on the recommendation of the Council of Ministers

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. According to **Article 169** of the Constitution of India, the creation or abolition of the Legislative Council in a state depends on the resolution passed by the Legislative Assembly of that state by a special majority and approved by Parliament by a simple majority.
This means that the **Parliament has the power to make or repeal a law regarding the existence of the Legislative Council in a state, but only if the states Legislative Assembly agrees to it by a two-thirds majority of its members present and voting**.
The law may also contain provisions for amending the Constitution as necessary to give effect to the changes in the state legislature.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Part V & VI: The Union and The States
questions.microTopic: Parliament Procedures, Proceedings, Officers, Bills, Committees, Houses, etc.
statementLines: The 'Contingency Fund' of the State is operated by-
questions.questionText: The 'Contingency Fund' of the State is operated by-

options.b: The Chief Minister of the State

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The **Governor of the State** can make advances from the Contingency Fund of the State, which is established under **Article 267 (2)** of the Constitution, to meet urgent unforeseen expenditure, pending authorisation by the State Legislature.
The corpus of the fund varies across states and the quantum is decided by the State legislatures. **

Important Tips-The **Contingency Fund of India** is a fund that is placed at the disposal of the President of India, who can make advances out of it to meet unforeseen expenditure pending its authorisation by the Parliament.
- The fund is administered by the Finance Secretary (Department of Economic Affairs) on behalf of the President of India and it can be operated by executive action.
- The fund was established under **Article 267 (1)** of the Indian Constitution and in **1950**, the Indian Parliament enacted the Contingency Fund of India Act **1950**.
- The fund was raised from Rs. 50 crores to Rs 500 crore in **2005**.

questions.subject: Polity

questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Which one of the following is not basic element of the citizen charter?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following is not basic element of the citizen charter?

options.a: Description of services being provided by department of the agency
options.b: Promotion of various methods to get benefit from the services available
options.d: Provision for the inspection of the agency's work

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. **Provision for the inspection of the agencys work is not a basic element of the citizen charter**.
A Citizens Charter represents the commitment of the Organisation towards standard, quality and time frame of service delivery, grievance redress mechanism, transparency and accountability.
The basic objective of the Citizens Charter is to empower the citizen in relation to public service delivery.
- Department of Administrative Reforms and Public Grievances in Government of India (DARPG) initiated the task of coordinating, formulating and operationalising Citizens Charters.
The Charters are expected to incorporate the following elements:
- Vision and Mission Statement Details of business transacted by the organisation Details of clients Description of services being provided by department or the agency.
- Details of grievance redress mechanism and how to access it Promotion of various methods to get benefit from the services available.
- Expectations from the clients Provide public records.

questions.subject: Polity
questions.sectionGroup: Institutions & Governance
questions.microTopic: Governance and Policies
statementLines: Which of the following is not true about the objectives of the Right to Information Act?
questions.questionText: Which of the following is not true about the objectives of the Right to Information Act?

options.a: To promote transparency and accountability in the working of the government
options.b: To make our democracy work for the people in real sense
options.c: To empower the women and weaker sections of the society

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. While the Right to Information Act, **2005**, aims to achieve various objectives, including promoting transparency, accountability, and empowering citizens, **it does not specifically mention the objective of empowering women and weaker sections of society**.
The Act primarily focuses on ensuring transparency and accountability in the working of the government, making democracy work for the people, and empowering citizens by providing them with the right to access information.
However, it does not explicitly mention empowerment of specific social groups such as women or weaker sections.

statementLines: Which of the following statement about resolution adopted by a State Legislative Assembly under Article 169 of the Constitution of India for the abolition of the State Legislative Council is correct?
questions.questionText: Which of the following statement about resolution adopted by a State Legislative Assembly under Article 169 of the Constitution of India for the abolition of the State Legislative Council is correct?

options.a: It does impose an obligation on the Governor to reserve resolution for the consideration of the President.
options.b: It does not impose an obligation on the Union Government to take action for initiating legislative in Parliament.
options.c: It does not impose an obligation on the Governor to reserve resolution for the consideration of the President.
options.d: It does impose an obligation on the Union Government to take action for initiating legislation in Parliament.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer. Under **Article 169** of the Indian Constitution, Parliament may by law provide for the abolition of the Legislative Council of a State having such a Council or for the creation of such a Council in a State having no such Council, if the Legislative Assembly of the State passes a resolution to that effect by a majority of the total membership of the Assembly and by a majority of not less than two-thirds of the members of the Assembly present and voting.
**It does not impose an obligation on the Union Government to take action for initiating legislation in Parliament**.

options.a: In a joint sitting of both Houses of Parliament.
options.b: In a joint meeting of both Houses of Parliament.
options.c: In a joint session of both Houses of Parliament.
options.d: Both Houses of Parliament assembled together.

questions.explanationMarkdown:

and (b) At the commencement of the first session of each year, the **President shall address both Houses of Parliament assembled together** and inform Parliament of the causes of its summons

3. The motion shall be restricted to a specific matter of recent occurrence.

Select the correct answer using code given below:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements are correct in reference to rule of procedure for Motion for Adjournment on a matter of public importance in Rajasthan Legislative Assembly? 1. Speaker's consent necessary to make motion. 2. The motion raises a question of privilege. 3. The motion shall be restricted to a specific matter of recent occurrence. 4. Not more than one such motion shall be made at the same sitting. Select the correct answer using code given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The rule of procedure for Motion for Adjournment on a matter of public importance includes:
- A motion for an adjournment of the business of the House for the purpose of discussing a definite matter of urgent public importance may be made with the **consent of the Speaker**.
- The motion should raise a matter which is definite, factual, urgent and of public importance.
- The motion should not cover more than one matter, and **should be restricted to a specific matter of recent occurrence**.
- The motion **should not raise a question of privilege**, nor should it revive discussion on a matter that has been discussed in the same session.
- **Not more than one such motion shall be made at the same sitting**.

options.a: Secure access to information from public authorities.
options.b: Provide information to the public through a person who has the information.
options.c: Provide information by Government on a public place.
options.d: Secure information by police from culprits.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The objective of the Right to Information Act is to **secure access to information from public authorities**.
The Act provides a mechanism for citizens to access information held by public authorities, ensuring transparency, accountability, and promoting good governance.
It empowers individuals to seek information and obtain records, documents, and other relevant information from public authorities. The Act aims to enhance citizen participation, promote informed decision-making, and hold public authorities accountable for their actions.

statementLines: Which of the following statements is/ are true in reference to the joint sitting of Parliament for passage of bill?
(i) Provision for Joint sitting is applicable for ordinary bills, financial bills and constitutional amendment bills
(ii) In no situation, Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha can preside over the joint sitting (iii) Quorum of I/6th of the total number of members of both houses to be maintained for joint sitting
Code:
questions.questionText: Which of the following statements is/ are true in reference to the joint sitting of Parliament for passage of bill? (i) Provision for Joint sitting is applicable for ordinary bills, financial bills and constitutional amendment bills (ii) In no situation, Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha can preside over the joint sitting (iii) Quorum of I/6th of the total number of members of both houses to be maintained for joint sitting Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect: Provision for Joint sitting is applicable for**only ordinary bills and financial bills**.** There is **no provision of joint sitting for deadlock on the Constitutional amendment bills**.
Constitutional amendment bill must be passed by each House separately.

**Statement 2 is incorrect: The Speaker of Lok Sabha presides over a joint sitting of the two Houses and the Deputy Speaker, in his absence.** If the Deputy Speaker is also absent from a joint sitting, the Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha presides.
If he is also absent, such other person as may be determined by the members present at the joint sitting, presides over the meeting. Hence, **Deputy Chairman of Rajya Sabha can preside over the joint sitting**.

**Statement 3 is incorrect: The quorum to constitute a joint sitting is**one-tenth (not one-sixth)**of the total number of members of the two Houses.** The joint sitting is governed by the Rules of Procedure of Lok Sabha and not of Rajya Sabha.

statementLines: In the following questions, there are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion and other is labelled as Reason (R): Assertion (A): The courts are barred from enquiring into the advice rendered by the ministers to the Governor.
Reason (R): There is no provision in the Indian Constitution for the system of legal responsibility of the ministers in the States.
In the context of the above two statements which of the following is correct?
questions.questionText: In the following questions, there are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion and other is labelled as Reason (R): Assertion (A): The courts are barred from enquiring into the advice rendered by the ministers to the Governor. Reason (R): There is no provision in the Indian Constitution for the system of legal responsibility of the ministers in the States. In the context of the above two statements which of the following is correct?

options.a: Both Assertion and Reason (R) are true, and Reason (R) is correct explanation of Assertion
options.b: Both Assertion and Reason (R) are true, but Reason (R) is not correct explanation of Assertion
options.c: Assertion is true, but Reason (R) is false
options.d: Assertion is false, but Reason (R) is true

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Assertion (A) is correct: **Article 163** of the Constitution of India reads: The question of whether any and if so what, **advice was tendered by Ministers to the Governor shall not be inquired into in any Court**.
Reason (R) is correct: There is **no provision in the Indian Constitution for the system of legal responsibility of the ministers in the States**.
Therefore, **Both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true but Reason (R) is the correct explanation of Assertion (A)**

statementLines: Match the articles related to State Legislature:
List -I
A. Constitution of legislature in States
B. The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly
C. Salaries and allowances of members
D. Definition of 'Money Bills' List-II
1. Article 178
2. Article 199
3. Article 168
4. Article 195 Code:
questions.questionText: Match the articles related to State Legislature: List -I A. Constitution of legislature in States B. The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly C. Salaries and allowances of members D. Definition of 'Money Bills' List-II 1. Article 178 2. Article 199 3. Article 168 4. Article 195 Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.
- Constitution of Legislatures in States: **Article 168** states that every state shall have a legislature consisting of the Governor and either one house (Legislative Assembly) or two houses (Legislative Council and Legislative Assembly).
- The Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the Legislative Assembly: **Article 178** states that every Legislative Assembly of a State shall choose two members of the Assembly to be the Speaker and Deputy Speaker, and fill the vacancies whenever they arise.
- Salaries and allowances of members: **Article 195** states that members of the Legislative Assembly and the Legislative Council of a State shall be entitled to receive such salaries and allowances as may from time to time be determined by the Legislature of the State by law.
- Definition of Money Bills: **Article 199** is about the definition of Money Bills. A Money Bill is a bill that deals with the allocation of funds, such as taxes, public spending, borrowing, and custody of the states funds.
**

Important Tips-A Money Bill can only be introduced in the Legislative Assembly of a state and can only be passed or rejected by it.
- The Legislative Council of a state can only make recommendations on a Money Bill within 14 days, but the Legislative Assembly can accept or reject them.
- The Speaker of the Legislative Assembly has the final authority to decide whether a bill is a Money Bill or not, and his decision is final.
- A Money Bill has to be certified by the Speaker before it is sent to the Governor for assent

statementLines: The merger of a political party with another political party in India is recognized in which of the following conditions?
questions.questionText: The merger of a political party with another political party in India is recognized in which of the following conditions?

options.a: When not less than one-third members of the legislature party have agreed to such merger.
options.b: When not less than half members of the legislature party have agreed to such merger.
options.c: When not less than two-third members of the legislature party have agreed to such merger.
options.d: When not less than three-fourth members of the legislature party have agreed to such merger.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. The recognition of a merger of a political party with another political party in India is subject to certain conditions as per the Tenth Schedule of the Indian Constitution, also known as the Anti-Defection Law.
According to the Anti-Defection Law, a merger of a political party with another party is recognized **if not less than two-thirds of the members of the legislature party have agreed to such merger**.
**

Important Tips If a member, after being elected as the presiding officer of the House, voluntarily gives up the membership of his party or rejoins it after he ceases to hold that office, he would not be disqualified on the grounds of Anti-Defection Law.
This exemption has been provided in view of the dignity and impartiality of this office.

statementLines: What will be happened if President summons for the joint session of Parliament and in the meantime, Lok Sabha dissolved?
questions.questionText: What will be happened if President summons for the joint session of Parliament and in the meantime, Lok Sabha dissolved?

options.a: Joint session will be held in scheduled time
options.c: Joint session will be held after composition of New Lok Sabha

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. Joint sitting is an extraordinary machinery provided by the Constitution to resolve a deadlock between the two Houses over the passage of a bill.
If the bill (under dispute) has already lapsed due to the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, no joint sitting can be summoned. But, the **joint sitting can be held if the Lok Sabha is dissolved after the President has notified his intention to summon such a sitting (as the bill does not lapse in this case)**.
After the President notifies his intention to summon a joint sitting of the two Houses, none of the Houses can proceed further with the bill.
**

Important Tips **Joint Sitting:**
- A deadlock is deemed to have taken place under any one of the following three situations after a bill has been passed by one House and transmitted to the other House:
- if the bill is rejected by the other House;
- if the Houses have finally disagreed as to the amendments to be made in the bill; or-if more than six months have elapsed from the date of the receipt of the bill by the other House without the bill being passed by it.
- In the above three situations, the president can summon both the Houses to meet in a joint sitting for the purpose of deliberating and voting on the bill.

statementLines: What is correct about the State Legislative Council?
(i) Its tenure is 6 years (ii) It is a permanent House (iii) It cannot be dissolved (iv) 1/6 members are elected by local institutions
(v) 1/6 members are elected by legislative assembly (vi) Every two years 1/3 members are retired
(vii) Deputy Governor is the Chairman of House (viii) The tenure of its members is 6 years
Code:
questions.questionText: What is correct about the State Legislative Council? (i) Its tenure is 6 years (ii) It is a permanent House (iii) It cannot be dissolved (iv) 1/6 members are elected by local institutions (v) 1/6 members are elected by legislative assembly (vi) Every two years 1/3 members are retired (vii) Deputy Governor is the Chairman of House (viii) The tenure of its members is 6 years Code:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is incorrect: The tenure of the State Legislative Council is not six years, but its members have a term of six years.**

**Statement 2 is correct: Unlike the State Legislative Assembly, which can be dissolved by the Governor on the advice of the Chief Minister, the**State Legislative Council is a permanent body**.** It is not subject to dissolution.
**Statement 3 is correct:**State Legislative Council cannot be dissolved**.** It continues to exist and functions as a legislative body even during times of political upheaval or government changes.
**Statement 4 is incorrect: One-third (not one-sixth) members of the House to be elected by electorates consisting of members of municipalities, district boards and such other local authorities in the State as Parliament may by law specify**

**Statement 5 is incorrect: One-third (not one-sixth) members to be elected by the members of the Legislative Assembly of the State from amongst persons who are not members of the Assembly**

**Statement 6 is correct: As per the Constitution,**one-third of the members of the State Legislative Council retire every two years**.** This arrangement ensures a continuous rotation of members and prevents a complete overhaul of the council at once.

**Statement 7 is incorrect: The State Legislative Council is presided over by a chairman who is not the Deputy Governor but is elected from among its members.** The Chairmans position is similar to that of the Speaker in the State Legislative Assembly.
**Statement 8 is correct:**The members of the State Legislative Council serve a term of six years**, and their tenure is not subject to dissolution during this period.** Members may retire, be replaced, or continue for the full six-year term depending on the rotation system.

statementLines: Privilege motion can be raised against a Minister when he-
questions.questionText: Privilege motion can be raised against a Minister when he-

options.b: Absents himself for a long time.
options.c: Withholds facts of a case or gives distorted versions of Facts.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Privilege motion** is concerned with the breach of parliamentary privileges by a minister.
It is moved by a member when he feels that a minister has committed a breach of privilege of the House or one or more of its members by withholding facts of a case or by giving wrong or distorted facts. Its purpose is to censure the concerned minister.
**

Important Tips **Motions:**
- No discussion on a matter of general public importance can take place except on a motion made with the consent of the presiding officer.
- The House expresses its decisions or opinions on various issues through the adoption or rejection of motions moved by either ministers or private members.
- The motions moved by the members to raise discussions on various matters fall into three principal categories:
- **Substantive Motion**: It is a self-contained independent proposal dealing with a very important matter like impeachment of the President or removal of Chief Election Commissioner.
- **Substitute Motion**: It is a motion that is moved in substitution of an original motion and proposes an alternative to it.
If adopted by the House, it supersedes the original motion.
- **Subsidiary Motion**: It is a motion that, by itself, has no meaning and cannot state the decision of the House without reference to the original motion or proceedings of the House.

Important Tips
- **President of India**: The President is elected by the members of an electoral college consisting of the elected members of both the Houses of Parliament and the elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States and the Union Territories of Delhi and Pondicherry.
- **Rajya Sabha members**: The Rajya Sabha, or Council of States, is the upper house of Parliament. It has 245 members, of which 233 are elected by the elected members of the State Legislative Assemblies using single transferable votes through Open Ballot.
- **Members of Legislative Council of the State**: One-third members of the Council are elected by members of the Legislative Assembly from among persons who are not members of either House of Parliament or either House of Legislature.

questions.questionText: Which one of the following statements is not correct?

options.a: A censure motion can be moved. against a single Minister of the Council of Ministers.
options.b: A motion of no-confidence cannot be moved against the Prime Minister alone.
options.c: A censure motion cannot be moved against the entire Council of Ministers.
options.d: A censure motion can be moved against a group of Ministers.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. A **censure motion** is a motion that expresses strong disapproval of the actions of a minister or group of ministers.
Censure motions can be moved against individual ministers, groups of ministers, or even the **entire Council of Ministers**. **

Important Tips Some other types of motion in the Parliament:
- **No-Confidence Motion**: A motion moved in the Parliament to test the support and confidence of the ruling government.
If the motion is passed, it indicates a lack of confidence in the government, potentially leading to its resignation.
- **Adjournment Motion**: A motion moved to suspend the regular business of the Parliament and discuss a matter of urgent public importance.
It leads to the adjournment of the current proceedings to address the specific issue raised in the motion.
- **Calling Attention Motion**: A motion moved by a member of Parliament to bring attention to an urgent matter that requires immediate government attention and response.
It seeks to elicit a response from the concerned minister.
- **Cut Motion**: A motion moved to reduce the amount of a demand presented in the budget.
It can be of three types: Policy Cut, Economy Cut, and Token Cut. It allows members to express their dissatisfaction or disagreement with the proposed expenditure.
- **Motion of Thanks**: A motion moved to express gratitude and appreciation to the President of India for their address to the joint session of Parliament at the beginning of a new session.

The seventh schedule provides for three list-State List, Central List, and Concurrent List. Out of the three list, the **Concurrent list includes subjects on which both the Parliament (Central government) and State Legislature (state governments) can legislate independently and concurrently**.
**

Important Tips Subjects under the **Central List**:
- Defense and armed forces-Foreign affairs and international relations-Atomic energy-Currency, coinage, and legal tender-Railways-Airports and airways-Interstate trade and commerce-Banking and financial institutions-Taxes on income other than agricultural income Subjects under the **State List**:
- Police and public order-Public health and sanitation-Agriculture and irrigation-Fisheries-Local government-Public works-Public entertainment-Land and land revenue-Education at the state level Subjects under the **Concurrent List**:
- Criminal law and procedure-Marriage and divorce-Adoption-Bankruptcy and insolvency-Education, including technical education-Population control and family planning-Protection of wildlife and animals-Trusts and trusteeship-Price control and essential commodities

A resolution supported by two-thirds of the members present and voting is required to be passed by Rajya Sabha to pass a law under the State list Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct -**Parliament can legislate a subject under the State list in certain conditions**. These conditions include during a national emergency under Article 250, when a state makes a request under Article 252, to implement international agreements under Article 253, and during Presidents Rule under Article 356.**
**Statement 2 is incorrect-Parliament can legislate a subject under the State list under certain circumstances during National Emergency and Presidents rule.** So, the statement that Parliament cannot legislate a subject under the State list under any circumstances is not correct.
**Statement 3 is correct-As per**Article 249**of the Constitution, if the**Rajya Sabha passes a resolution supported by not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting**, stating that it is necessary or expedient in the national interest, Parliament can make laws on any matter enumerated in the State list.**

Only Deputy Speaker vacates his office and Speaker shall not vacate his office until immediately before the first meeting of the Lok Sabha after dissolution. Select the correct answer using the codes given below:
questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct-Article 85(2) of the Constitution of India states that on dissolution of the House of the People, all seats in the House shall become vacant.** This means that **both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker, who are members of the Lok Sabha, will also cease to be members of the House on dissolution**.
**Statement 2 is incorrect-The**Speaker does not vacate his office on dissolution of the Lok Sabha**.** He continues to hold office until immediately before the first meeting of the House after dissolution.

statementLines: Which one of the following does not fall under the definition of the Money bill?
questions.questionText: Which one of the following does not fall under the definition of the Money bill?

options.a: Amendment of law with respect to any financial obligations undertaken by the government of India
options.b: The payment of money into the Consolidated Fund of India
options.c: Any financial bill as per requirements of Article-117
options.d: Appropriation of money out of the Consolidated Fund of India

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option c is the correct answer. **Financial Bills** are a broader category that includes Money Bills as well as bills that deal with any other financial matters.
Financial Bills are dealt with under Articles 117 (1) and Article 117 (3.) Though Money Bill is a species of the financial bill however, not all financial bills are money bill.
**

Important Tips **Financial Bills (I)**
- They are bills that contain matters related to finances, including matters beyond the scope of a Money Bill (Article 110).
- They are introduced in the Lok Sabha and require the Presidents recommendation.
- Unlike Money Bills, they can be rejected or amended by the Rajya Sabha.
- In case of a deadlock, a joint sitting can be summoned. The President has the authority to give assent, withhold the bill, or return it for reconsideration.
**Financial Bills (II)**
- They solely deal with provisions involving expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India and do not include any matter of a Money Bill (Article 110).
- They are treated as ordinary bills in all respects. These bills cannot be passed by either house of Parliament unless recommended by the President.
- They can be rejected or amended by either House, and a joint sitting can be summoned in case of a deadlock.
- The President has the power to give assent, withhold the bill, or return it for reconsideration.

**Explanation:** Option b is the correct answer.

**Statement 1 is correct:**Short notice questions**allow Members of Parliament or Legislators to seek immediate clarification or information from the government on pressing issues.** These questions can be asked for oral answer at a notice period **less than 10 days**.
**Statement 2 is correct: In the case of short notice questions, the**concerned Minister has the discretion to accept or reject the question**.** If the Minister agrees to answer, the question becomes admissible and can be taken up for discussion during the session.

statementLines: Which one of the following is NOT true of the 10th Schedule of the Constitution of India pertaining to disqualification of members of the Parliament and State legislatures?

options.a: The schedule lays down that elected members may be disqualified on the grounds of defection.
options.b: Disqualification on grounds of defection does not apply in cases of merger with another political party.
options.c: Cases of dispute are decided by the speaker or Chairman of the House concerned.
options.d: The Supreme Court of India is the final arbiter in cases which remain unresolved.

questions.explanationMarkdown:
**Explanation:** Option d is the correct answer. When there is a dispute regarding the disqualification of a member on grounds of defection, the **Speaker or Chairman of the House makes the decision** and not Supreme Court, however the decision of the Speaker or Chairman is subject to judicial review.
**

Important Tips **Grounds for Defection**
- **Voluntary Give Up**: If an elected member voluntarily gives up his membership of a political party.
- **Violation of Instructions**: If he votes or abstains from voting in such House contrary to any direction issued by his political party or anyone authorized to do so, without obtaining prior permission.
- As a pre-condition for his disqualification, his abstention from voting should not be condoned by his party or the authorized person within 15 days of such incident.
- **Independently Elected Member**: If any independently elected member joins any political party.
- **Nominated Member**: If any nominated member joins any political party after the expiry of six months.

**Explanation:** Option a is the correct answer. The correct sequence of different stages a budget has to go through in the Parliament is as follows:
